judge_details.raw:
I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the stated constraints and requirements.

---

## CONSTRAINT CHECKLIST

**Hard constraints:**
- ≤500 words (body text)
- Exactly 4 sections: Scope, Permitted Uses, Prohibited Uses, Enforcement
- Each section has numbered items (not prose paragraphs)
- Every prohibition references which base fact motivates it
- Enforceable without new tooling
- No aspirational language

---

## PROPOSAL 1 ANALYSIS

**Structure:** 4 sections ✓, numbered items ✓

**Word count:** The body text is substantially over 500 words. Counting conservatively, the Prohibited Uses section alone exceeds 300 words. Total body text is ~550–600 words. **Likely fails the 500-word constraint.**

**Prohibition basis citations:** All 6 prohibitions cite base facts ✓

**Aspirational language:** None detected ✓

**Enforceability without new tooling:** Uses PR process, SOC2 incident channels, quarterly access reviews ✓

**Content issues:**
- Permitted Use #3 flags "license headers or license-referencing comments" for legal review, but Prohibited Use #3 says *all* AI-generated code requires legal review before commit regardless of headers. These are contradictory — the Permitted Use implies a narrower trigger while the Prohibition is universal.
- Prohibited Use #2 (manager co-signature on all external AI content) is operationally very burdensome and may not be enforceable in practice, but it does use existing approval chains.
- Slack prohibition basis is well-reasoned.
- The 40 unseated engineers prohibition is addressed explicitly ✓

**Notable:** Proposal 1 includes a preamble section before the analysis — wait, no, Proposal 1 is just the policy document. It's essentially identical to Proposal 2's policy section.

---

## PROPOSAL 2 ANALYSIS

**Structure:** 4 sections ✓, numbered items ✓

**Word count:** The document includes a lengthy preamble ("Changes made and which problems they fix") before the policy itself. If we count only the policy body, it's the same text as Proposal 1 (~550–600 words). **The policy body likely fails the 500-word constraint.** The preamble also adds significant length and is not part of the memo.

**Same internal contradiction as Proposal 1:** Permitted Use #3 flags license headers for legal review, but Prohibited Use #3 requires legal review for *all* AI-generated code. Contradictory.

**Prohibition basis citations:** All 6 cite base facts ✓

**Aspirational language:** None ✓

**Enforceability:** Same as Proposal 1 ✓

**Additional issue:** The preamble is not part of the policy memo — it's meta-commentary. This is extraneous material that shouldn't be in a policy memo submission.

---

## PROPOSAL 3 ANALYSIS

**Structure:** 4 sections ✓, numbered items ✓

**Word count:** Counting body text: Scope (~50w), Permitted Uses (~100w), Prohibited Uses (~150w), Enforcement (~70w) ≈ ~370 words. **Passes the 500-word constraint ✓**

**Prohibition basis citations:**
- Prohibition 1: Cites Incident 1, DPA, SOC2, GDPR, FedRAMP ✓
- Prohibition 2: Cites Incident 2 ✓
- Prohibition 3: Cites Incident 3, outside counsel ✓ — BUT the prohibition says "if the code includes any license header or license-referencing comment," which is narrower than the base fact warrants (outside counsel flagged all AI-generated code for copyrightability, not just code with visible headers). **Weak/incomplete.**
- Prohibition 4: Cites only "Company Slack is an approved tool with AI features currently disabled" — this is the weakest citation. It doesn't reference the DPA risk, the three incidents, or the compliance obligations. **Fails the "reference which base fact motivates it" requirement adequately.**
- Prohibition 5: No explicit basis citation. **Fails the requirement.**

**Aspirational language:** None ✓

**Enforceability:** Uses existing PR process, SOC2 channels, quarterly reviews ✓

**Content issues:**
- Permitted Use #2 says sales "may use AI tools for internal drafts only" — but no AI tool for sales has been approved. This creates a permission that doesn't exist in the base facts.
- "rewritten" in Permitted Use #2 and Prohibited Use #2 is unverifiable — there's no existing process to confirm rewriting occurred. Less enforceable than the co-signature approach.
- Missing explicit prohibition on the 40 unseated engineers using other AI tools.
- FedRAMP escalation path not addressed in Enforcement.
- Prohibition 5 lacks a basis citation entirely.

---

## COMPARATIVE SUMMARY

| Criterion | Proposal 1 | Proposal 2 | Proposal 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| ≤500 words | ✗ (marginal over) | ✗ (over + preamble) | ✓ |
| 4 sections, numbered | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| All prohibitions cite base facts | ✓ (all 6) | ✓ (all 6) | ✗ (P4 weak, P5 missing) |
| No aspirational language | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Enforceable without new tooling | ✓ | ✓ | Partial (rewriting unverifiable) |
| Internal consistency | ✗ (P.Use 3 vs. Proh. 3) | ✗ (same) | ✗ (P.Use 2 permits unapproved tools) |
| Covers 40 unseated engineers | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| FedRAMP escalation | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| No extraneous content | ✓ | ✗ (preamble) | ✓ |

**Proposals 1 and 2** are substantively identical in policy content. Proposal 2 adds a meta-commentary preamble (not part of the memo) and is therefore strictly worse than Proposal 1 as a submitted document. Both share the same internal contradiction (Permitted Use #3 vs. Prohibited Use #3) and likely exceed 500 words.

**Proposal 3** passes the word count but has more substantive failures: missing/weak basis citations on two prohibitions, an unenforceable "rewriting" standard, a permitted use that implies approval of unapproved tools, and missing coverage of the 40 unseated engineers and FedRAMP escalation.

**Best:** Proposal 1 — most complete coverage, all prohibitions cited, FedRAMP addressed, 40-engineer gap covered, no extraneous content. Word count is close to the limit and the internal contradiction is a flaw, but overall the most complete and rigorous policy.

**Second:** Proposal 3 — passes word count, clean structure, but has meaningful gaps in prohibition citations, enforceability, and coverage.

**Worst:** Proposal 2 — same substantive flaws as Proposal 1 plus extraneous preamble content that doesn't belong in a policy memo.

RANKING: 1, 3, 2

I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the stated constraints and requirements.

---

## CONSTRAINT CHECKLIST

**Hard constraints:**
- ≤500 words (body text)
- Exactly 4 sections: Scope, Permitted Uses, Prohibited Uses, Enforcement
- Each section has numbered items (not prose paragraphs)
- Every prohibition references which base fact motivates it
- Enforceable without new tooling
- No aspirational language

---

## PROPOSAL 3 ANALYSIS

**Word count:** The body is roughly 380–400 words. Passes.

**Structure:** 4 sections, all numbered. Passes.

**Prohibition basis citations:** 
- Prohibition 1: ✓ cites Incident 1, DPA, SOC2/GDPR/FedRAMP
- Prohibition 2: ✓ cites Incident 2
- Prohibition 3: ✓ cites Incident 3 and copyrightability concern
- Prohibition 4: *Weak.* Cites only "Company Slack is an approved tool with AI features currently disabled" — this is circular (it's disabled because it's disabled). No reference to DPA risk, the three incidents, or compliance obligations. This is the weakest citation in any proposal.
- Prohibition 5: ✓ references Legal approval process

**Prohibition 3 scope problem:** "Engineers submit all AI-generated code through the existing legal review checklist before commit **if the code includes any license header or license-referencing comment**." This is a significant flaw — it limits the review trigger to visible license headers, missing the broader copyrightability concern flagged by outside counsel (which applies to all AI-generated code).

**Permitted Use 2 problem:** "Sales and other functions may use AI tools for internal drafts only" — but no AI tool for sales has been approved. This implicitly permits unapproved tools for internal use, which contradicts the base facts and Prohibition 5.

**"Rewritten" enforceability:** "a human has rewritten it" is unverifiable with existing controls. The constraint says enforceable without new tooling.

**40 unseated engineers:** No prohibition or guidance for the 40 engineers without Copilot seats. This is a gap — 40 engineers have no approved tool but the policy doesn't explicitly prohibit them from using alternatives.

**Aspirational language:** None detected. Passes.

**FedRAMP:** Mentioned in Prohibition 1 basis but not in Enforcement. Minor gap.

---

## PROPOSAL 2 ANALYSIS

**Word count:** Body text is approximately 430–450 words. Passes.

**Structure:** 4 sections, all numbered. Passes.

**Prohibition basis citations:**
- Prohibition 1: ✓ Strong — cites Incident 1, DPA, SOC2/GDPR/FedRAMP
- Prohibition 2: ✓ cites Incident 2
- Prohibition 3: ✓ cites Incident 3 and copyrightability broadly (not limited to visible headers)
- Prohibition 4: ✓ cites the 80/120 seat gap and lack of DPA/IP review
- Prohibition 5: ✓ cites all three incidents, data handling obligations, DPA concern — much stronger than Proposal 3's Slack prohibition
- Prohibition 6: ✓ references Legal approval

**Prohibition 3 scope:** Correctly applies to ALL AI-generated code, not just code with visible license headers. Stronger than Proposal 3.

**Sales tool gap:** Permitted Use 2 explicitly states "no approved AI tool for external drafting" for sales — closes the gap Proposal 3 left open.

**40 unseated engineers:** Prohibition 4 explicitly addresses this. Stronger than Proposal 3.

**"Rewritten" issue:** Proposal 2 uses "manager co-signs or approves the communication in writing" — this is verifiable via existing email/approval chains. More enforceable than Proposal 3's "rewritten" standard.

**FedRAMP in Enforcement:** Enforcement item 2 explicitly addresses FedRAMP escalation. Addresses a gap present in Proposal 3.

**Aspirational language:** None detected. Passes.

**Enforceability without new tooling:** All mechanisms reference existing PR processes, SOC2 incident channels, quarterly access reviews, email approval chains. Passes.

**Filler:** Minimal. The basis citations are substantive, not padding.

---

## PROPOSAL 1 ANALYSIS

Proposal 1 is structurally identical to Proposal 2 in its final policy text — the differences are only in the preamble, which explains revisions made. The policy body is word-for-word the same as Proposal 2.

**The preamble itself:** The task asks for a policy memo, not a revision memo with a preamble. The inclusion of "Changes made and which problems they fix" is extraneous and not part of the deliverable. This adds words that count toward the 500-word limit if the evaluator counts them, and more importantly, it's not what was asked for — it's meta-commentary, not the memo itself.

**Word count with preamble:** The preamble adds ~200+ words. If counted, the total far exceeds 500 words. Even if the preamble is excluded, the policy body is the same as Proposal 2.

**Policy quality:** Identical to Proposal 2 where it counts.

**Structural issue:** Presenting a revision rationale rather than a clean memo is a format failure relative to the task.

---

## COMPARATIVE SUMMARY

| Criterion | Proposal 1 | Proposal 2 | Proposal 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| ≤500 words (policy body) | Pass (body only) | Pass | Pass |
| Format as requested memo | Fail (has preamble) | Pass | Pass |
| 4 sections, numbered | Pass | Pass | Pass |
| All prohibitions cited | Pass | Pass | Weak on Slack |
| Prohibition 3 scope (all AI code) | Pass | Pass | Fail (header-only trigger) |
| Sales tool gap closed | Pass | Pass | Fail |
| 40 unseated engineers addressed | Pass | Pass | Fail |
| Enforceability of external content check | Pass (co-sign) | Pass (co-sign) | Weak ("rewritten") |
| FedRAMP in Enforcement | Pass | Pass | Partial |
| No aspirational language | Pass | Pass | Pass |

**Proposal 2** is the cleanest execution: correct format, no extraneous content, all constraints met, stronger than Proposal 3 on multiple dimensions.

**Proposal 1** has identical policy content to Proposal 2 but is presented with a non-requested preamble that violates the spirit of the task (deliver a memo, not a revision log) and potentially the word count.

**Proposal 3** has three substantive policy gaps (Prohibition 3 scope, sales tool ambiguity, unseated engineers) and a weak Slack prohibition citation.

---

RANKING: 2, 1, 3

I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the stated constraints and requirements.

## Constraint Checklist

**Constraints:**
- Maximum 500 words (policy body only, presumably)
- Exactly 4 sections: Scope, Permitted Uses, Prohibited Uses, Enforcement
- Each section must have numbered items (not prose paragraphs)
- Every prohibition must reference which base fact motivates it
- Enforceable without new tooling
- No aspirational language

---

## Proposal 3 Analysis

**Structure:** 4 sections ✓, numbered items ✓

**Word count:** The policy body is approximately 420 words. ✓

**Prohibitions with basis citations:**
- P1: Cites Incident 1, DPA, SOC2/GDPR/FedRAMP ✓
- P2: Cites Incident 2 ✓
- P3: Cites Incident 3, copyrightability concern ✓
- P4: Cites "company Slack is an approved tool with AI features currently disabled" — **this is weak**. The basis doesn't reference the actual risk (DPA, data handling, the incidents). It just restates the current state, which is not a motivating fact for a *prohibition*.
- P5: No basis citation at all ✗

**Aspirational language:** "Sales and other functions may use AI tools for internal drafts only. AI-generated text is not sent externally until a human has **rewritten** it" — "rewritten" is vague and unverifiable, making this potentially unenforceable. ✗

**Coverage gaps:**
- 40 unseated engineers: Not addressed. 80 seats for 120 engineers — what do the other 40 do? No prohibition or guidance. ✗
- P3 scope is too narrow: "if the code includes any license header or license-referencing comment" — this misses the broader copyrightability concern that applies to ALL AI-generated code, not just code with visible license headers. ✗
- Permitted Use #2 implicitly allows sales to use unspecified AI tools for internal drafts, but no tool is approved for sales. This creates a contradiction. ✗

**Enforcement without new tooling:** Mostly ✓, but "rewritten" standard is unverifiable.

**No aspirational language:** Mostly clean ✓

---

## Proposal 2 Analysis

**Structure:** 4 sections ✓, numbered items ✓

**Word count:** Policy body approximately 430 words. ✓

**Prohibitions with basis citations:**
- P1: Cites Incident 1, DPA, SOC2/GDPR/FedRAMP ✓
- P2: Cites Incident 2 ✓
- P3: Cites Incident 3, copyrightability concern, and correctly broadens scope to ALL AI-generated code ✓
- P4: Cites the seat gap (80/120) and lack of DPA review ✓
- P5: Cites all three incidents, data handling concerns, DPA terms ✓ (much stronger than Proposal 3's Slack prohibition)
- P6: No explicit basis citation ✗ (though implied)

**Coverage gaps:**
- 40 unseated engineers explicitly addressed in P4 ✓
- P3 correctly applies to all AI-generated code, not just code with license headers ✓
- Sales tool gap addressed: Permitted Use #2 explicitly states no approved tool for sales ✓
- "Manager co-signature" as enforcement mechanism is verifiable using existing email/approval chains ✓

**Aspirational language:** None detected ✓

**Enforcement without new tooling:** Manager co-signature uses existing email; SOC2 incident channels; quarterly access reviews already required. ✓

**FedRAMP addressed:** P2 in Enforcement specifically escalates FedRAMP-relevant violations ✓

**No preamble/meta-commentary:** The document is clean — just the policy. ✓

---

## Proposal 1 Analysis

**Structure:** 4 sections ✓, numbered items ✓

**Content:** Essentially identical to Proposal 2 in the policy body. The difference is that Proposal 1 includes a lengthy preamble explaining "changes made" — this is meta-commentary, not part of the policy itself.

**Word count:** If counting only the policy body, approximately the same as Proposal 2 (~430 words). However, the full submission is much longer due to the preamble. The task says "maximum 500 words" — if this applies to the full submission, Proposal 1 fails. If it applies only to the policy memo itself, it passes.

**Quality of policy content:** Identical to Proposal 2 in substance.

The preamble in Proposal 1 is extraneous — the task asked for a policy memo, not a revision explanation. This is unnecessary filler that either violates the word count or clutters the submission.

---

## Comparative Ranking

**Proposal 2** is the cleanest and most complete:
- All prohibitions have strong basis citations
- 40-seat gap addressed
- P3 correctly scoped to all AI-generated code
- "Manager co-signature" is verifiable
- No meta-commentary
- No aspirational language
- FedRAMP escalation addressed
- Only minor gap: P6 lacks explicit basis citation, but it's a catch-all

**Proposal 1** has identical policy content to Proposal 2 but includes a lengthy preamble that is not part of the memo and inflates word count. The policy itself is equally strong, but the submission as a whole is worse due to the extraneous material.

**Proposal 3** has the most substantive flaws: weak Slack prohibition basis, P3 too narrowly scoped (only license-header-triggered review), P5 missing basis citation entirely, the 40-seat gap unaddressed, and the "rewritten" standard is unverifiable/aspirational.

RANKING: 2, 1, 3
